data.title: eBay: Improving And Fully Re-Rated
data.description:
By year-end 2023, I concluded that cheap was not enough in the case of eBay Inc. (EBAY). Amidst stagnant revenues and earnings, and some key engagement metrics on the decline, a cheap valuation and plan to simply buy back stock felt not enough to get involved.

Cheap valuations in the end provided a foundation for a meaningful outperformance, although some heavy lifting was done by the business as well, which has seen accelerating growth. Subsequently, shares have more than doubled from levels in the low forties to the $90 mark here, as eBay has delivered and convinced investors along the road.

This means that shares have risen for the right reasons, but the valuation multiple has re-rated substantially, which makes me much more reserved now, as further triggers here require more and continued growth for longer.

eBay: The Marketplace

eBay is known as a marketplace for consumers and businesses (both acting as buyers and sellers) to make a wide variety of transactions between consumers, with businesses, or between businesses. The platform remains dominant for peer-to-peer and SMB trade, including 134 million active buyers, about $80 billion in gross merchandise volume, including truly global operations.

This is easily seen if we look at the financial numbers over the past decade, as revenues have risen by just about two billion to just over $10 billion, with this growth not keeping up with inflation. Meanwhile, the company has seen quite stable operating margins in the mid-twenties, as the real achievement has been unheard-of buybacks, with the share count down over 60% over the past decade.

The lack of operating performance invited Elliott Management to get involved in 2019, pushing eBay to sell some business assets along the way, as that has been part of the reason for the shortfall in sales. This included a more than $9 billion sale of eBay's Classified business, after which eBay sold its stake in the Korean business in another $3 billion deal as well. The proceeds came on top of net cash balances held by the business already.

2023 Perspective

With reported results held back by the divestment of the abovementioned assets, the company saw some stagnation. In November 2023, the company reported a 2% increase in third quarter GMV to $18 billion, with quarterly revenues up 5% to $2.50 billion and earnings just topping the $1 per share mark.

Factoring in the fair value of the shares held in Adevinta (to whom eBay sold its Classified business), net cash holdings rose to $2.3 billion. With 532 million shares trading at $41 per share, the market value came in near $22 billion, with operating assets valued around $20 billion.

This made it so that operating assets traded at 8-9 times earnings, yet these earnings were adjusted, among others, excluding $600 million in stock-based compensation expenses. If we back this out, realistic earnings multiples for the operating assets came in around 11-12 times earnings.

That looked really cheap, yet the business was shrinking in terms of relevance and engagement, all of which made me cautious at the time.

Making Inroads

That conclusion has been too conservative with the benefit of hindsight, as shares rose to the $60 mark in 2024, the $80 mark in the summer of 2025, and even to a high around $100 in the fall. By now, shares have pulled back to $90 per share at this moment.

In the spring of last year, it was evident that eBay had seen an improvement in some operating performance. Total gross merchandise volumes for the year 2024 rose 2% to $74.7 billion, but growth accelerated throughout the year. Reported revenues rose by a similar near 2% to $10.3 billion for the year. GAAP operating profits came in at $2.3 billion, with GAAP earnings reported at $3.99 per share, as non-GAAP earnings were reported at $4.88 per share, that number up an impressive sixty-four cents on an annual basis.

Net cash holdings are down to just below a billion, mostly because of large share buybacks, the main purpose of the capital allocation scheme of the past decade.

The big gains were seen in 2025, as the operating performance revealed 2%, 4%, and 8% constant currency growth in both GMV and sales in the first three quarters of the year, respectively. The company attributes this to savvy usage of AI, as well as various other business initiatives.

Consequently, the company sees currency-neutral sales up around 6% to $11.0 billion in 2025, based on GMV growth of around 5% to levels just over $79 billion. Adjusted earnings are seen around $5.45 per share, with GAAP earnings seen just over $4.10 per share. With about three-quarters of the adjustments relating to stock-based compensation expenses, I see realistic earnings of $4.50 per share here, as I do not accept that adjustment. Continued buybacks resulted in the company having incurred a modest net debt load, now seen around half a billion.

The 467 million shares trading around the $90 mark grant equity a $42.0 billion equity valuation and slightly higher enterprise valuation. This made it so that valuations have risen to about 4 times sales, just below that, and with reported earnings around $4.50 per share, these shares trade around 20 times earnings. This makes it so that 120% share price returns over the past two years have largely been driven by valuation multiple inflation. Operating asset earnings multiples increased from around 11-12 times realistic earnings to 20 times, and only to a minor extent by earnings improvements and the impact of buybacks.

However, some momentum is definitely seen returning to eBay which has long stagnated but is really seeing 8% growth here, driven by improvement of the marketplace function. The composition of this engagement is interesting, as the number of active buyers is only up a percent to 134 million users, while GMV is up 10%, driven by strength in the US operations and thus driven by higher activity per user.

What Now?

The discussion above really shows what is going on. Based on a non-demanding valuation, amidst concerns about the platform's relevance, shares have seen a huge re-rating over the past two years. Concerns came after GMV rose just 2% in 2023 in an inflationary period, all while the number of active buyers was actually down. Lower engagement and activity was in part due to greater competition, including Facebook's Marketplace, among others.

This came after credits were due where they are due, as eBay has stepped up its game this year, with accelerating growth seen here. From a very low valuation base, any kind of accelerating business momentum deserves a re-rating. By now, shares have risen to 20 times realistic earnings. This looks reasonable for a business with very minimal net leverage, also given that the business has seen 8% growth in recent times. However, it seems hard to believe that 8% revenue growth (organic) seems attainable in the years to come.

Given all this, I feel no need to chase the shares after this great run-up. While the business is better and the valuation a re-rating seems more than deserved, I am cautious. After all, current growth momentum is welcomed, but any setbacks in this, which could easily happen, might easily put pressure on valuation multiples awarded to the business. This makes me cautious to build real confidence in the investment case yet, also as the re-rating has been rather aggressive.

I admit to too cautious an approach before, seeing no need to get involved just yet.

data.title: Powell Under Attack
data.description:
Deja vu, as the Fed's Chair, Jerome Powell, gave an announcement that the Fed (and he specifically) is under an investigation prompted by the executive office. The Fed was hit with DOJ subpoenas relating to their historic building renovations, which have cost some $2.5B so far, something President Trump has insisted was beefed up fraudulently. This saga has been ongoing all year, with President Trump continuing to volley insults and threaten to fire Chair Powell all through 2025. This investigation appears to be the next step forward in the threats.

The outcome that President Trump wants? Ostensibly, control of interest rate policy.

In the past, President Trump has said that he wants Powell out because the Fed has been too slow on rate cuts and that rates should be far lower today than they are. Back in June, President Trump had his press secretary present a note to the public on this matter, comparing the Fed Funds Rate to other countries and suggesting that the US should have a rate sub-1% immediately.

Powell, in his own statement, said that he believes this is tactical:

I have deep respect for the rule of law and for accountability in our democracy. No one—certainly not the chair of the Federal Reserve—is above the law. But this unprecedented action should be seen in the broader context of the administration’s threats and ongoing pressure.

This new threat is not about my testimony last June or about the renovation of the Federal Reserve buildings. It is not about Congress’s oversight role; the Fed through testimony and other public disclosures made every effort to keep Congress informed about the renovation project. Those are pretexts. The threat of criminal charges is a consequence of the Federal Reserve setting interest rates based on our best assessment of what will serve the public, rather than following the preferences of the President.

I have a feeling that my readers' stance on this issue will fall along political lines (as it often does when President Trump intercedes in monetary policy matters), but what won't be deniable is the impact on markets.

Treasuries Rise, Dollar Hurts

The core reaction to this kind of meddling was summed up in a joint letter written by the past three Fed chairs, Yellen, Bernanke, and Greenspan, "This is how monetary policy is made ⁠in emerging markets with weak institutions, with ‍highly negative consequences for inflation and the functioning of their economies ‌more ‌broadly."

Markets have become somewhat accustomed to these kinds of stunts by now, and the reactions are far more muted than they were back in June when Powell made those remarks to Congress that are now being called into question. Rhetoric then about firing Powell was just as bellicose, but the market reaction was far more extreme.

Equity markets were mixed, but this announcement did a few things. Notably, it snapped an uptrend in the dollar. Predictably, this dollar fall came at the same time as a jump in high-trust currencies like the Euro and Swiss Franc.

This was accompanied by a rise in interest rates along the entire curve, which tends to happen when the dollar weakens but also is a show of confidence in the government's management of the economy more broadly.

The US 30yr is now at 4.84%, still sitting around its highest levels in years.

The consequence of attacking the central bank's members and it as an institution is a loss of trust in both the central bank and the executive branch. Regardless of the side one falls on in who is right, Trump or Powell, they will leave the discussion likely having a worse opinion of the integrity of the other party. This distrust for either side squarely harms the credibility of the US monetary system as a whole. That creates demand for higher interest rates on Treasuries and less demand for the dollar.

This fight is one more reason for foreign countries to de-dollarize their treasuries, which is what I am most concerned about in this exchange. The dollar didn't take too bad of a hit, but gold and silver ran up quickly.

A Precious Metals Victory

The clear winner of this exchange is precious metals. On the day of the news, gold shot up over 2% and silver over 7%. They have been experiencing their own rallies in a broader sense, largely due to central bank buying, but this is another catalyst for foreign countries to get a move on and get rid of more dollars.

Investors flee to safety when things get tense, and bonds are precluded from this since they are trying to flee to safety away from the government. As long as this showdown is on between Powell and Trump, it is wise to be long precious metals; they already are and are likely to stay as the undisputed benefactor of any erosion of Federal Reserve independence.

It's still likely that Powell will resign his governorship when his term of Chair ends in May, but he could choose to stay on as a governor until January 2028. If he decides to stay, and the odds are looking better that he will following this incident, he could be a thorn in the administration's side for a while and drag on this issue through the courts, which could last longer than Jan. 2028.

Any future clashes are likely to be met with the same fate: an erosion of trust in the US by market players. That comes with all the trappings that one gets in emerging markets: weaker currency, higher rates for sovereign bonds, and a flight away from your currency and bonds into precious metals and other currencies.

Will This Change the Fed Decision?

To President Trump's chagrin, I find this move unlikely to motivate the Fed to move any faster on their current rate path. They have been very adamant that the executive branch does not decide monetary policy and that they will move at the pace they find best. Powell reiterated this in his statement on the investigation. I doubt this will motivate any change in their decision-making.

I still expect rates to be held at the next FOMC meeting, coming at the end of the month, as the Fed enters its next "wait and see" period. We are unlikely to get another rate cut, from my view, until at least April, if not June, when we have a new Fed chair.

Regardless of what happens with the investigation, Powell will step down as chair in May. Another Fed chair will be announced, with the front-runners being Kevin Warsh and Kevin Hassett, although I believe that Waller is the best candidate to promote both unity and dovishness, a win-win for both the FOMC and executive view. Although I'm willing to admit that he doesn't have great odds for a reason, President Trump's history and current actions with this investigation suggest that he does not care to please the FOMC and choose an insider.

Conclusion

Ultimately, I'm not sure what will come of the investigation into Chair Powell or the Fed's renovations; history with the Lisa Cook investigation tells us that it's unlikely that Powell would be removed from his role unless found criminally liable, but by the time any case could be fully made against him, he will be out of his role. The law simply doesn't move fast enough to fire him now, even if this one does stick.

I am sure that the longer this saga goes on, the less trust institutions and investors will have in the US. That leads to a weaker dollar overall, higher rates on Treasuries, and continued bullish pressure on precious metals.

The response to this event was fairly muted compared to what it was months ago when things like this happened. Perhaps investors, equities traders in particular, are getting used to it and are already pricing in that the investigation won't yield anything usable or that the Supreme Court will rule that Powell can stay in his position, like they did for Cook. It seems to me that this event was not the catalyst I expected, and the market is pricing in a retraction of this event. Either way, broken trust cannot be repaired as easily.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Hurco Closing Out A Brutal Down-Cycle, End-Market Signals Remain Mixed
data.description:
Market conditions continue to be a slog for Hurco (HURC), as this small manufacturer of machine tools for largely small/mid-sized manufacturing customers continues to see weak demand amid significant uncertainty and caution among its customers. Although there are some signs of an upturn in multiple shorter-cycle manufacturing end-markets, a V-shaped recovery seems unlikely at this point. Hurco shares have been lackluster since my last update, with the shares down about 14% and underperforming the broader industrial sector by about 20 percentage points. DMG Mori (MRSKF) has done even worse (down around 20%), while other manufacturers of “machines that make machines” (like Andritz (ADRZY) have done better, but still worse than the broader industrial space.

At this point, I believe Hurco has seen the worst of the cycle, but with global geopolitical and macroeconomic uncertainty running high, it’s difficult to have a lot of confidence in that view. Moreover, Hurco doesn’t show the signs of a small company successfully innovating, disrupting, or taking share in its core market, so it’s hard to call this more than a cyclical recovery play.

A Soft Close To A Weak Year

Hurco’s fiscal fourth quarter report capped the company’s third straight year of year-over-year revenue declines – something that hasn’t happened in over 20 years – and results were not strong.

Revenue fell 17% year over year in constant currency terms, though only slightly on a sequential basis. Revenue performance was mixed across its major geographic regions. Sales in North America (about 40% of sales) were down 22% year over year but up 8% sequentially, while sales to the EU (a little more than 50% of sales) were down 12% year over year in constant currency, and also down about 3% sequentially. Sales to Asia-Pacific were even weaker, down 25% yoy and 19% qoq, but this has never been a large part of the business.

Gross margin continued to weaken, falling 570bp year over year and 290bp quarter over quarter to 17%, the worst quarterly result in over 15 years. Margin continues to be hurt by a lack of overhead leverage, but also a mix shift away from higher-value, higher-margin systems. SG&A spending fell more than 10% from the prior year, but operating loss still widened to $3.5 million from $0.5 million last year and $3.1 million in the prior quarter.

Relative to my model, North American sales were weaker than I’d expected in the second half of the year, while European sales were stronger. Gross margin ended up about 50bp weaker than I expected in the second half, with a roughly $3 million greater EBITDA loss. A bigger decline in net working capital (driven mostly by inventory) drove a stronger-than-expected free cash flow result for the full year, and the company ended the year with over $6/share in cash.

Conflicting Signals Don’t Inspire A Lot Of Confidence

Unfortunately, there are not many companies like Hurco – the publicly traded machine tool companies out there are quite a bit larger, tend to sell to larger multinational manufacturers, and, in the case of Japanese machine tool companies, don’t sell as much into Europe. Still, I think it’s possible to make some broad observations based on Hurco’s reported results, other company reports, and macro/sector commentary.

Looking at Hurco, orders were down 11% yoy in the quarter, but up 13% qoq, with North American orders up 4% yoy and 30% qoq, while EU orders were down 17% yoy and 14% qoq.

DMG Mori reported a 7% sales decline in its Q3 (DMG Mori’s third quarter ended September 30, while Hurco’s quarter ended October 31), bringing its year-to-date sales performance down 11%. Orders were flat year-to-date, but up 16% yoy and 4% qoq in the third quarter.

Comparing these order trends to reported machine tool orders offers a little more nuance. U.S. manufacturing technology orders were up 40% in October and up 17% year-to-date through September, but this includes more than just machine tools. Meanwhile, third-quarter machine tool orders in Germany were down 15%, while they were up 12% in Italy (Germany and Italy are two significant markets for Hurco).

Broadening out, PMIs remain weak across Hurco’s major markets, with the most recent U.S. manufacturing PMI at 47.9 (below 50 indicates contraction), though the S&P’s measure (a broader survey that includes smaller companies) came in at 51.8. In Germany and Italy the PMIs were sub-50 (47.0 and 47.9), while the U.K. moved up to 50.6. So all in all, I would say that the overall manufacturing environment is still not particularly robust.

Given that Hurco’s prime customer base is smaller manufacturers, I think it’s worth looking at some other indicators for activity there. The NFIB’s small business economic trends report for November showed that only 20% of respondents indicated that they planned to increase capital outlays and only 15% expected the economy to improve, and the numbers were broadly similar from August to October for capital outlays.

Fed surveys likewise suggest lackluster activity, with the Philly, Richmond, and Dallas Feds all describing weak activity with muted capital investment and excess capacity. Last but not least, the most recent senior loan officer opinion survey suggests flattish small business demand for loans, and overall C&I lending was weaker in the fourth quarter relative to the third quarter and down year-over-year in December.

Okay, so what does all of this mean? I believe these various reports reflect a still-challenging and still-cautious environment for smaller manufacturing companies. Demand really hasn’t picked up, and with uncertainty on demand, interest rates, tariffs, and so on, it seems smaller companies are being cautious with capex spending and expansion. To that end, I’d note that Hurco disclosed that underlying machine volumes were up, but sales were down, suggesting that customers were trading down to less sophisticated machinery.

The Outlook

Given how weak this down-cycle has been for Hurco, I have real and growing concerns about the company’s ongoing competitiveness in the market. There are already challenges in the machine tool space generally – manufacturers in China and Korea have become more competitive, and manufacturers are shifting more toward near-net-shape processes, which reduces their need for machine tools.

At a minimum, I’d like to see Hurco keeping up with the industry at large, and I have increasing concerns on this front. While it’s admittedly difficult to compare Hurco to large players like DMG Mori, Mazak, Okuma (OKUMF), Makino (MKMLF), and so on, and it’s reasonable to expect a supplier to smaller firms to see more volatility and bigger up/down swings across the cycle, I am nevertheless concerned about the longer-term trends in revenue and margins here.

Looking at recent reports from companies like Kennametal (KMT) and MSC Industrial (MSM), particularly the latter’s recently reported fiscal first quarter, conditions appear to be getting better, but there’s no evidence of a V-shaped recovery in the immediate future.

I’m looking for Hurco’s revenue to rebound by about 8% in FY’26, with more momentum in the second half of the year, and a return to double-digit growth in the year after. This is a stronger rebound than I expect in the broader manufacturing sector but is predicated on smaller companies being slower to resume investment spending and then making up for lost time. I’m looking for long-term revenue growth closer to 5%, but that’s off of a cyclical bottom, and the true run-rate long-term revenue growth is more in the 2% to 3% range.

I’m not confident that Hurco will generate positive full-year EBITDA next year, but I do expect it in the year thereafter. Long term, I expect low single-digit free cash flow margins, with good years in the high single-digits and low double-digits.

I’ve taken a more conservative approach to modeling given the longer-term trends that appear to be at play, and with that, the longer-term DCF-based annualized prospective return is more in the mid-to-high single digits. I’ve likewise cut my EBITDA multiple based upon weaker margin trends, but a 6.5x multiple on adjusted ‘27/’28 EBITDA (discounted back) does still support a high-teens to low-$20’s fair value.

The Bottom Line

This is a hard call today. On the one hand, I have much less confidence in Hurco as a company capable of creating real long-term value in the machine tool space. That alone may well be disqualifying for some investors. On the other hand, I do see leverage to an eventual cyclical recovery here, and Hurco’s leverage to smaller customers could give it outsized upside to that recovery, assuming smaller businesses participate.

While I do not have confidence in Hurco as a long-term holding, selling a cyclical industrial company at or near the bottom of a cycle feels short-sighted, and I think there’s cyclical upside provided that the broader manufacturing sector does recover meaningfully as 2026 plays out.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Unusual COMEX Trend Could Signal Accelerating Silver Squeeze
data.description:
By Mike Maharrey

An interesting development in the COMEX silver exchange seems to reflect an accelerating silver squeeze.

The stage was set for a silver squeeze last April when significant amounts of metal moved from London to New York, driven by tariff worries. The displacement of metal, coupled with surging Indian demand, set off the first squeeze in October. That drove the silver price over $50 for the first time.

Metal flowed back to London, but that didn’t solve the underlying problem. While shuffling silver between London, New York, and India took the immediate pressure off the market, it didn’t magically create new silver, and it didn’t take long for silver squeeze 2.0 to develop last month. That pushed the price over $80.

This squeeze isn’t manifesting just because metal is in the “wrong place.” The underlying issue is that there are true shortages beginning to manifest.

Silver demand has outstripped supply for four straight years, and the Silver Institute projects that 2025 will be the fifth. The structural market deficit came in at 148.9 million ounces in 2024. That drove the four-year market shortfall to 678 million ounces, the equivalent of 10 months of mining supply in 2024.

This inventory tightness globally seems to have created an unusual setup in COMEX futures, as investors appear to be moving March contracts backward to January and February.

Analysts came to this conclusion by examining the open interest data released by the COMEX.

Open interest represents the total number of outstanding contracts. In other words, these are contracts that have been created but not yet closed out or settled by delivery or cash settlement at expiration.

Examining the January 7 data, we find that open interest in the January 2026 contract rose by 1,431. Open interest in the February 2026 contract also rose by 1,564 contracts. That brings the total open interest contracts for those months to 2,995.

Meanwhile, the March 2026 open interest fell at a nearly identical number – 2,936.

The best explanation for this surge in open interest for earlier contracts and the sudden drop in March open interest is that investors are rolling contracts backward. In other words, they are exchanging positions from a longer contract into earlier contracts that are closer to the expiration date.

Why would they do this?

Simply because they want to take delivery of the metal now, even though January is not typically an active delivery month. They don’t want to wait until March.

Keep in mind, there could be other reasons for these moves; however, this seems to be the most logical interpretation of the numbers.

Jon Lindau explained the situation in a report on SilverTrade.com.

“Typically, rolls are forward (to the next front month) in normal markets, as traders wish to maintain a position in silver (exposure to the silver price), without taking physical delivery of the metal.

"Backward rolling (moving the position from the front month March to nearer months, as seen here) indicates the exact opposite: backwardation along with immediate scarcity of physical silver metal as traders wish to take delivery instead of merely remaining exposed to the futures price of silver.”

Lindau said two scenarios could be driving backwardation.

“Longs positioning aggressively to stand for immediate delivery, which will further drain COMEX registered stocks and further exacerbate the silver shortage.

"Conversely, if shorts are involved in the roll, they might be attempting to avoid delivery demands in March by shifting earlier, but this would still be extremely dangerous for shorts amid tight supply and increasingly more contracts standing for delivery in January.”

Again, there could be other reasons for the recent shift in open interest. However, Occam's Razor teaches us that the simplest explanation is generally the best. It's at least the best place to start.

It appears we are reaching a point where those with long positions don't want to wait too far into the future to take possession of physical metal they may want or need.

As Lindau put it, if this is what's happening, the COMEX could end up in big trouble.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The Bullish Trend Everyone Is Waiting For, But Nobody's Looking At
data.description:
By Geoff Bysshe

While most analysts and the media are focused on the 2 primary market-driving economic data points - the labor market and inflation - there’s another series that will likely determine how the market responds to employment and inflation data in 2026.

Ironically, few investors are talking about it. Some are even denying it’s real, but as you can see in the chart below, it’s taking off and could push stocks much higher for years, just like it did in the late 1990s.

However, one data series (that doesn’t get a lot of attention) isn’t going to fuel a bull market alone. So, before we dive into this obscure economic data, let’s review the economic data that is on investors' minds.

Two weeks ago, in this column titled "The New Bull Market Formula How to Keep The Bull Market Strong," I outlined a framework for identifying stocks that are most likely to experience a strong bull trend. At the core of that framework is the belief that it takes several different kinds of trends to align to get a big move.

While those same characteristics can be used to anticipate the moves in the equity indexes, there is a similar framework that can be applied to understanding “what kind of economy” we’re experiencing. Different flavors of economic growth will have different outcomes for the stock market.

This is why the market is so focused on the “nuance” of the labor market being described as a “no hire, low fire” weakness versus a labor market where firing and layoffs are the focus.

As always, I’ll suggest that this nuance is less important than how the market reacts to the labor data report. So let’s start there.

Was Friday’s Unemployment Data Bullish?

Last week, the market focused on the employment report. The labor market is the biggest reason the Fed would continue cutting rates. The immediate response to the unemployment rate being slightly better than expected (lower) led many market strategists to conclude that the Fed would not lower rates in January.

In the recent past, such a conclusion would have likely sent stocks and bonds lower. Instead, the market’s initial premarket reaction was slightly positive.

A slightly higher open in the regular session was followed by a brief selloff, which tested the bulls' resolve and created the Opening Range reversal pattern (which we teach as a “how to buy the dip” strategy), and then rallied confidently into the afternoon.

In the afternoon, the broader indexes, IWM and RSP (S&P 500 equal-weighted), took a breather, while the big caps continued to climb for the rest of the day.

Friday’s trading pattern is one I wish all our members and readers knew well, as it offers low-risk entries for day traders, swing traders, and investors. It also gives you insight into the strength of the bigger trends - especially when it is a reaction to an important news event like last Friday's.

It’s even more powerful when we’re in a bullish January Trend Trade condition, which was described as “what to watch out for” in last week’s Market Outlook titled “Watch Out For The Market’s 2026 Balancing Act."

The January Trend trade is in full gear after last week, and this coming week, it will hit an important milestone (we’ll discuss that below). But first...

I’ve explained Friday’s bullish intra-day pattern in the image below.

This Pattern Is Not Just For The Indexes!

Last week’s article highlighted Rocket Lab (RKLB) as a stock in a bullish condition, and almost every day last week, it had a version of the pattern above. Instead of gapping up and pulling back, it was pushed lower right on the open, but then found support around the OR Low and FTP zones and rallied for the rest of the day.

What’s So Important About Friday’s Bullish Response To The Unemployment Report?

The consensus of market strategists on Friday was that the Fed will not cut in January. With that narrative in the market, stocks rallied in a very bullish way. Why?

The market probably rallied for three reasons.

  1. The market’s expectations (based on the Fed Fund futures market) for even one rate cut before Powell leaves in May have been decreasing for weeks. There would have had to have been a negative surprise on Friday to change that trend. That said, the report lowered expectations for a rate cut before June, while at the same time increasing the odds of a June cut.
  2. The price action leading up to the report was such that the market had paused for two days after a strong start to the year. The nervous bulls had already sold, or were waiting for the report (probably the latter).
  3. The market is in a bullish January Trend Trade. So, cautious bulls were able to continue buying after the employment news was not a negative surprise. **The reason I come to this conclusion is the market action described above. It’s not because anyone really knows what the market will do before it happens!**

The January Trend Trade Gets More Bullish

Last week’s Market Outlook shared the following image with comments on the upcoming week, our January Trend trade would follow the direction of a breakout of the Friday, January 2 range.

This week, these charts show how the market has moved relative to that first-day range.

Some noteworthy observations:

  1. SPY broke out and retraced back to the Calendar Range OR high on Thursday, before hitting new highs on Friday.
  2. QQQ struggled to get over its January OR high, but it closed over it.
  3. DIA broke out and continued nicely
  4. IWM has the most bullish breakout because it has a pattern we look for to identify the best trends - not trading under a prior-day low.

What’s Next For The January Trade?

The January Trend Trade is just getting started. In fact, the big range that we will follow all year will not be set until this Thursday.

On Thursday morning in our mentoring room, we will be looking at the stocks and sectors with January Calendar Ranges that are positioned for big moves.

It’s not uncommon for stocks that got off to a hot or bearish start to stall or reverse this week. We’ll look for those patterns to identify Calendar Range reversals and breakouts.

The simple application is to trade in the direction of the price trend relative to the range you’re focused on. The tactics are in knowing the best ranges and the confirmation rules for breakouts and reversals.

The Economic Trend Investors Should Watch in 2026

Last week, Jonathan Levin wrote a great article for Bloomberg on productivity. It focused on the probable sources of the high rate of productivity in the economy and its potential impact on Fed policy.

The economist side of me spent hours gathering data on this point, but the trader in me left it all on the cutting room floor to avoid complicating a simple relationship, since the market will ultimately tell us how to focus on this data.

There are several economic relationships that will determine the importance of reported “economic proactivity” in how it will impact stock prices, but those are details.

Jonathan summarized what I’d like to emphasize here:

“The US economy seems to be in the throes of one of its biggest bursts of productivity in decades, weighing on business labor costs and hastening the disinflation process. If artificial intelligence is partly the reason for the gains, then it’s plausible that we’re in the early days of durable improvements to efficiency.”

This “burst” of productivity can be seen in the chart below, created by Ed Yardeni, one of the best market strategists on the street, who does a great job at following productivity as it relates to the market.

Note the acceleration during the early 1980s (a huge bull market), then in the late 1990s (dot-com bull market), and now the similar trajectory (AI-driven bull market).

Warning: Increased productivity does not guarantee a bull market, for several reasons.

However, one condition under which increased productivity will likely lead to a bull market is when its impact boosts corporate earnings.

“AI” is a very broad term used to describe many very different technologies, so it’s often hard to put it in a simple descriptive box. Consider AI to be a whole new category of technological tools that has already demonstrated its ability to reshape how we live, work, produce new physical products, develop new ideas, and more.

It’s undeniable that AI tools and technology can make a person much more productive. The charts above and below suggest that it is making the economy more productive.

The title of the chart below from Jonathan’s article suggests that the high productivity is "as good as it gets."

The math behind this calculation may make it difficult for this chart to "break out" like a stock chart, but there is a very plausible way that this chart could get much better. All it has to do is stay at its current level. This wouldn’t just be better than "as good as it gets" - it would be exponentially better.

The simple reason to believe that we’re not at the end of an AI bubble (we may be in one) is demonstrated by the chart below (also from the Bloomberg article).

For all the hype in the media about AI, the productive use of it is extremely low relative to its near-term potential.

If the productivity data is as strong as it is at this point, given this level of AI adoption, there are much bigger gains ahead. These gains won’t always be announced in the media, but they’ll show up in earnings growth, better margins, more efficient cost structures, and more.

Several months ago, the market would have sold off on the prospect of the Fed slowing or pausing rate cuts.

Last Friday, stocks rallied like they were confident they'll deliver the earnings they'll need to keep the bull market going.

The 2026 market feels like watching a parent teaching a child how to ride a bike without training wheels for the first time. The market feels like it’s at the moment when the parent isn’t holding the bike, and the child is riding on his own without realizing it.

Let’s hope this earnings season doesn’t tip the market over.

If productivity is any indication, I think we’re in good shape.

Big View Summary

By Keith Schneider

Every week, we review the big picture of the market's technical condition as seen through the lens of our Big View data charts.

The bullets provide a quick summary organized by conditions we see as being risk-on, risk-off, or neutral.

Summary: Overall risk-on conditions strengthened as major indexes pushed to new highs led by small caps, market internals and new-high/new-low readings confirmed broad participation, volatility stayed near lows, and leadership expanded into metals, value, foreign equities, and multiple “Modern Family” groups. Offsetting this optimism are still-mixed volume patterns, lagging growth versus value, elevated leadership in defensive metals and gold alongside rising oil, and a few cross-asset signals (SPY/GLD, rates, crypto) that warrant monitoring for early warning signs if growth weakens.

Risk-On

  • Indexes were up strong this week, led by small caps up over 4.5%. Three of the four have closed on all-time highs in bull phases and aren’t overbought on real motion. We see some healthy rotation out of the concentrated themes from last year. (+)
  • Sectors were up nearly universally (XLU the only sector down). (+)
  • The biggest leaders in the market this week were metals, with palladium, silver, gold miners up significantly. Some from geopolitical stress, but also infrastructure and technology demand. (+)
  • Cumulative advance decline line (hit new all-time highs) and market internals turned positive. (+)
  • New high new low ratio is stacked and sloped positive. (+)
  • The color charts (moving average of stocks above key moving averages) is bullish for SPY and IWM and more mixed on QQQ. (+)
  • Risk gauges flipped back positive, with the SPY/GLD ratio the only one showing risk-off. (+)
  • Volatility remains low, with cash volatility near its all-time lows. (-)
  • New high in value, while growth is still lagging, though both are in bullish phases. Value is running a little rich in - terms of price momentum. If growth starts to drop below the lows of last week, it could be a concerning or early warning sign. (+)
  • The Modern Family is strong across the board, with some new highs this week and everything in bull phases. (+)
  • Foreign equities look to be in a strong uptrend without getting overbought and outperforming the U.S. (+)
  • Soft commodities aren’t showing much inflationary pressure. (+)
  • January seasonals tend to be very strong, typically led by Nasdaq with good participation in S&P and IWM. (+)

Neutral

  • Volume patterns remain mixed, with strength in DIA and IWM, while much weaker in SPY and QQQ. (=)
  • The biggest leaders in the market this week were metals, with palladium, silver, gold miners up significantly. Some from geopolitical stress, but also infrastructure and technology demand. (+)
  • Gold remains strong and oil started to move this week. Demand for energy is there along with persistent geopolitical stress. (=)
  • Bitcoin did get a little pop this week and looks better on the weekly chart. (=)
  • Rates had a muted response to the employment numbers and remain in a channel. (=)

Actionable Trading Plan

Market posture: Stay risk-on but not maxed out. Breadth, new highs, and global strength support staying invested, while mixed volume and metals leadership argue for discipline.

What to own now

  • Keep core exposure in broad equity and small-cap baskets to capture expanding participation.
  • Overweight value/cyclical groups and foreign equities while they continue to lead.
  • Hold a tactical sleeve in metals/miners, but manage them actively.

What to be careful with

  • Growth/tech positions stay on a tighter leash until leadership improves.
  • Avoid concentrating in last year’s crowded winners.

What to do this week

  • Add on market digestion days, not strength.
  • Rotate, don’t chase - shift capital toward areas showing improving participation and away from fading themes.
  • Rebalance exposure weekly based on breadth, leadership, and risk gauge alignment.

Risk controls

  • Keep overall exposure slightly below full risk-on.
  • Begin trimming if growth weakens further, metals continue to dominate leadership, or breadth/new highs lose confirmation.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: DOJ Vs. Powell: We Have A Valuation Problem (And 2 Strategies For Now)
data.description:
The FED And Its Independence

The FED seems under attack, and this time Jerome Powell wanted to clearly state that the DOJ is investigating him as "a consequence of the Federal Reserve setting interest rates based on our best assessment of what will serve the public, rather than following the preferences of the President". At stake is the FED's independence.

This is where the escalation between the FED and the government seems to have reached a point of no return, creating a particular environment that can be discussed in many ways. In this article, we will focus on the implications for the market and us as investors, avoiding any political angle (there are appropriate places on Seeking Alpha to discuss politics).

This is particularly interesting because the U.S. was born by demanding independence, and the idea of "checks and balances" is deeply rooted in its institutions.

The FED was created in 1913, after the 1907 bank run. It was independent but still closely linked to the Treasury. For example, during the Great Depression, President Roosevelt and Congress mandated that the FED issue reserve notes that were not backed by gold, even though, at that time, the gold standard was effective. Eventually, the Fed, too, won its independence after WWII in a famous clash against President Truman. During the war, the Fed kept interest rates at around three-eighths on Treasuries. Once the war was over, the FED wanted to go back to a normal interest rate policy, which was particularly needed as inflation was high (over 20% in 1951). President Truman wanted low rates, but the FED didn't agree, and this went on until the "Treasury-Fed Accord of 1951" was signed, which began the era of the FED's independence in deciding monetary policy without having to consider debt management policies. Finally, in 1977, Congress approved the Federal Reserve Reform Act, which explicitly gave the FED the dual maximum employment/stable prices mandate that it still has today.

There have been other times when the Fed's chair and the President clashed (Johnson vs. Martin in 1965; Nixon vs. Burns in the early 70s; Bush Sr. vs. Greenspan in 1991-1992). Sometimes, the Fed has been the scapegoat that presidents used to blame for a cooling economy. But today's clash seems similar to what happened with Truman, and the outcome could even be a regime shift. While it is too early to speculate on that, I would like to focus on the value of the FED's independence, not from a moral or political point of view, but from the standpoint of investors. In other words, do investors pay a premium for this independence? Do they value it?

The Value Of The FED's Independence

Now, there is no FED ticker. So, what we are going to do is to understand how the FED's independence is embedded in key prices that make sense only if we assume that the FED is free to follow the monetary policy it wants without any interference from outside.

When we look at the 5-yr expected inflation, we see that, last month, we were at 2.24%, which is actually quite close to the FED's target of 2%. This is even more striking as inflation is right now stabilizing around 3%, and the 1-year expected inflation is at 3.2%. In other words, it seems as if the markets still believed (before the investigation, of course) that the FED would have obviously stuck to its 2% inflation goal. If we see a sudden spike in inflation expectations before a true reacceleration in the next two or three CPI prints, then we would see that markets are probably fearing a political interference affecting the FED.

Some may say that this is just about expectations and that there is no real threat to our investments. Not really, I would argue. When inflation expectations move up, you see that the nominal yields and required returns are also impacted. Everything is, in a certain way, repriced because investors increase the return rates they demand to protect themselves from being destroyed by high inflation.

The first signal came from today's gold price (let's use the ETF GLD as a proxy), which was up 2.4% in a move that I consider a true reaction to the news. When inflation expectations are influenced by politics, investors seek refuge in gold. In particular, while bonds can tell us what the market expects from the FED and the yield required to lend money to the government, gold simply tells us whether investors feel or not that the FED can truly pursue its goals and deliver accordingly. As the "higher-for-longer" narrative grew, leading many to believe that we won't see 2% for inflation for several years, gold - unsurprisingly - started rallying, fueled also by the numerous geopolitical crises around the world that make it stand out as a safe haven.

Secondly, when expected inflation ticks higher, nominal yields go up as well. This implies that bond prices move down. Usually, if expected long-term inflation moves up by 1 percentage point, we can see a drop in long-duration bond prices between 8% and 15%. This is simply how the math of repricing works.

Third. If inflation expectations move up, the risk-free rate also does. The equity premium also goes up because investors price more political uncertainty. The outcome is that investors use higher discount rates, which is bearish for valuations. Remember what happened in 2022, when investors realized that inflation was too hot? We had a bear market (by the way, it was a great moment to buy).

Fourth. Admitting that the FED protects its independence, when inflation expectations trend higher, the FED is usually forced to extend its restrictive policy, fueling the higher-for-longer scenario.

Who pays? Bonds and unprofitable companies whose profits are out in the future. Who wins? Not many. Gold (and safe havens). Perhaps short-duration credits. But even businesses with pricing power and superb quality could be brought down by a renewed bear market.

This explains why inflation expectations are extremely valuable for investors. They should never be overlooked.

We are accustomed to seeing analysts use the 10-year Treasury yield as the risk-free rate. However, an earthquake took place in May 2025 when Moody's downgraded the U.S. debt. This is when the valuation authority professor, Aswath Damodaran, explained that we could not think of U.S. Treasuries as completely risk-free. It was not a matter of political or philosophical beliefs; it was a matter of looking at how the market was reacting and starting to reprice the equity risk premium for U.S. stocks, although by a little.

Strategic Implications

1. Gold: The Anti-Political Asset

Let's go back to gold. In a normal environment, Gold usually trades inversely to real interest rates, meaning that when inflation is high, gold moves up, and when inflation is low, gold usually decelerates. But as we move on, we are learning (or we are simply rediscovering) that gold can be priced as an asset that has no counterparty. Let me explain what this means. If the U.S. government charges the Fed chair and threatens him with jail, then risk-free assets, such as U.S. Treasuries, start having a political connotation. They carry a political weight. This is because Treasuries have a counterparty. On the other hand, gold has none, and investors like this. It also seems that central banks around the world, the so-called whales, believe gold is better than Treasuries right now, and this is why we are seeing countries such as China or Poland buying gold hand over fist. Clearly, in this attempt to diversify and reduce their exposure to the U.S. dollar, they are pushing gold prices to the moon. But the clash between the FED and the DOJ will only bring additional fuel to this belief, incentivizing further divestiture from the U.S. currency.

Now, while we saw gold catching investors' eyes as inflation was too high after the pandemic, we also have to realize that the reason why investors kept holding gold has developed and changed over time. Right now, it is "politically risk-free", and this seems to matter more compared to what inflation will do from now on. Basically, when we are buying gold, we are now buying a premium caused by distrust in political institutions.

In this environment, holding gold could be a bet on further institutional disruption. As far as I am concerned, I won't be part of this move. I see it as a real bet, instead of an investment decision, because it relies on too many unknowns. Furthermore, as much as I like buying gold rings and jewels for my wife (besides their beauty, I like that they usually don't go down in value), I usually want to invest in real cash-producing assets. In the long run, I am a firm believer that the power of labor and innovation is a better way to grow the value of my investments rather than relying on commodity price fluctuations. Gold is in a league of its own, but there are plenty of tables showing that over long periods of time, stocks are the best way to go. Some may say that gold miners could be a better choice because they lagged the metal, and they will have to eventually catch up. It could be a reasonable take, because if political risk keeps being high or even rises more, then prices will remain extremely high and will eventually show in many companies' earnings. But, if you have followed my reasoning, investors are looking for hedges. Miners are not. Therefore, I doubt that they will attract big inflows of money as gold itself is doing right now.

2. Short-Treasuries

If the Fed is going to lose its independence (it won't take just one investigation from the DOJ to achieve this goal, but what happened does push in this direction), then, as we saw above, expectations around inflation could soar. This would imply seeing 10-yr Treasuries tank as their yields move up. Long-term bonds are simply to be avoided. This is why short-term Treasuries could become more interesting (SHV). Right now, they yield around 4.2%, and because of their maturity date, they have low sensitivity to a change in rates. We can see it clearly below. Once the Fed finished increasing interest rates, SHV has simply been trading within a well-defined range. This means that short-term Treasuries are almost completely immune to duration risk. We know that the clash between the President and the Fed chair is because of interest rates, with the former wanting a dovish policy. Right now, trying to guess where long-term rates will fall is extremely dangerous because we don't know how things will develop.

Let's assume that the Fed will see its independence somewhat weakened. This means that it will probably cut rates more than it would like to. As a result, long-term rates will probably rise because they will factor in a renewed inflation risk. The yield curve will therefore steepen, and this is bad for long-term bonds because it pushes their prices down. Holding short-term Treasuries is a way to earn a decent yield on cash while giving us the opportunity to avoid guessing correctly what the curve will do. SHV is what some could call a preserver of optionality. It keeps our capital intact and allows us to have flexibility when it comes to timing our next investments.

As some may know, I like to always have some cash aside. Until now, I had some accounts where my cash was earning 5%. But now the yield has moved down, and SHV is the likeliest candidate I am considering to park the liquidity I hold that I want to eventually deploy in the market when opportunities arise. SHV is not truly an investment; it's not about having a bullish or bearish outlook on the market (by the way, I am bullish), but it prevents us from paying a high price if we make the wrong bet on where rates will fall.

Conclusion

We are really in the early stages, even though this is the peak of an ongoing institutional clash (or friction). However, we are now at a point where the financial system is going through a stress test. The market has been used to seeing a well-defined barrier between monetary and fiscal policies. If this is blown away, the market will take its toll. Currently, we are seeing some signs of an increasing political risk priced into U.S. equities. Risk-free is changing its meaning, and it is becoming more and more linked with gold rather than U.S. Treasuries. Gold is there to prove it to us. In addition, I would never consider cash as trash right now. It is an investment in flexibility that earns us a very decent return while allowing us to make the best investing decisions. While I am a big fan of seeking return on capital, sometimes I believe that being sure that, first and foremost, the return of capital is 100% guaranteed is a short-term scenario that we have to endure, whether we like it or not.

I am not saying that we should be bearish on the market. As I have often explained, I am actually bullish and believe 2026 will reward some clear winners while also paving the way for earnings growth breadth across many sectors. For those who are a bit scared, I could also suggest a much more relaxing and calming piece where I explained that there are at least 5 things that won't change in 2026, offering us very compelling investing opportunities without having to worry every day about headlines. But, surely, in the light of this new development, I would consider keeping some dry powder aside to be able to make the most out of sudden opportunities that open up.

data.title: SMDV: Why I Still Like It
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome The Intelligent ETF Investor as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

I started writing about exchange-traded funds (ETFs) at Seeking Alpha last year, but I have been following the space for a very long time. I am a big believer in ETFs! They appeal to individual investors and also to many investment managers and advisors. Low costs, high liquidity, and the idea of buying an index make sense, though there are many ETFs that fall outside the passive index mode.

I wrote about ProShares Russell 2000 Dividend Growers ETF (SMDV) in early November, and it has rallied. My initial view was bullish, and I still like it and am updating my view on SMDV today.

The SMDV Price Action

Since the initial piece I wrote in early November was published, SMDV has gone up. The ETF had a rough 2025. Here is the three-year chart:

In 2025, the ETF had a total return of 0.3%, with the dividends overcoming the 2.4% price decline. The stock is still below its late 2023 peak and well above the low set in late 2024. The ETF had gapped up in late November, but a pullback during trading on 12/31 filled that gap.

As I discussed in that last piece, the ETF, which is focused on small caps with good dividend growth, has performed poorly relative to large-caps. Here is the longer-term perspective of SMDV and the Russell 2000 (IWM) and the S&P 500 (SPY):

Over the past decade, SMDV has lagged SPY, but it has also lagged IWM. This lag is fully explained by the market action since stocks were hit hard in early 2020 following the pandemic arriving in America.

I created an ETF Focus List that currently has 78 ETFs, several of which are dividend-related. I have written about some of these at Seeking Alpha and had Sell ratings on Schwab U.S. Dividend ETF (SCHD) and Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF Shares (VIG). Here is how SMDV has performed against several other dividend ETFs over the past three years (since 12/31/22):

Of these dividend-focused ETFs, SMDV has outpaced only one, Invesco High Yield Dividend Achievers ETF (PEY) and has lagged the other five. The total return is less than half that of VIG. SMDV has an expense ratio of 0.4%, which seems reasonable to me though a lot higher than many of these other ETFs.

SMDV Composition

In the last piece, I discussed several factors about SMDV, including its year of inception (2015) and the manager, ProShares, which runs 50 ETFs, including eight that are dividend-focused. SMDV is the third-largest and S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats ETF (NOBL) is the largest. I wrote about this one recently, rating it Hold, though I do include it in my ETF model portfolio and my wife did add it this week.

SMDV is the only ETF focused on the dividend growers in the Russell 2000. Most of the dividend-focused ETFs and funds are focused on large-caps. The ProShares website for SMDV shares the holdings, which include 104 stocks with a current assets under management of $637 million. Here is the look at the top names:

I like that the fund has equal weightings of its members when it rebalances. Here is the ETF by sector:

The ETF has a lot of exposure to Financials, Industrials and Utilities, and I like that it has very little exposure to Information Technology. Here are some characteristics of SMDV and the other six dividend-focused ETFs that I had in the chart above as well as SPY:

SMDV is focused on small-caps, and it is small. It is also not widely followed. The contrarian in me digs this!

Why I Still Like SMDV

While small-caps and SMDV have rallied recently, I still like the ETF at this higher price. I like small-caps. That doesn't mean that I will always like that part of the market or every single small-cap, but small-caps have performed poorly relative to large-caps for a while now. This has happened as some large-caps have done exceptionally well. The S&P 500 has gotten very concentrated in just a few names and has picked up a large amount of exposure to the Technology sector. I like that SMDV is well diversified and has little exposure to the large S&P 500 names.

As I mentioned when I initiated coverage of SMDV, the M&A environment has gotten better, and this could be good for the small-caps in the ETF.

I understand why people like dividend ETFs, and they have done very well compared to this one. The Sell ratings that I issued for VIG and for SCHD in October suggested cash or TIPS as alternatives, but that doesn't do it for many investors. I think that SMDV is an excellent replacement for these and other dividend ETFs that are large-cap focused.

Conclusion

I like SMDV despite its recent rally. I created a model portfolio for ETFs at year-end, and SMDV was one of the six ETFs included (at a weight of 15%). I realize that the ETF has jumped since I first called it out and in 2026 so far, but it looks cheap to me. I don't see any chart challenges, with the stock still well below the late high in 2024. The focus on small-caps, the higher cost than other funds, and the small size scare off some, I suppose, but this bearish analyst thinks it can hold in better if stocks weaken or perhaps rally a lot if they don't.

data.title: American Express Downgraded As Trump Floats Credit Card Rate Cap
data.description:
President Trump attacked not only Fed Chair Powell in recent months, but over the weekend, he took verbal aim at credit card companies. The POTUS floated the idea of capping credit card interest rates at 10%, which, if made law, would significantly hurt earnings for the likes of American Express (AXP), Visa (V), and Mastercard (MA).

Indeed, shares of those companies cratered on Monday, Jan. 12, with AXP being hurt the most. Today, ahead of its January 30 Q4 earnings report, I am downgrading AXP from a "Buy" to a "Hold." I was upbeat on the valuation and technicals of the now $252 billion market cap Financials sector company back in November 2023.

Shares are up a whopping 155% since then, more than doubling the S&P 500’s return. But the valuation is now stretched, while the technicals are not wholly bullish.

AXP Outperforming XLF and SPY YoY

Back in October, AXP reported a solid set of quarterly results. Q3 non-GAAP EPS of $4.14 beat the Wall Street consensus forecast of $3.98, while revenue of $18.4 billion, up 11% from the same period a year ago, was a material $380 million beat. The management team hiked its full-year 2025 revenue guidance from 9% to 10%, while seeing operating EPS in the range of $15.20-$15.50. Provisions for credit losses fell 5% YoY.

Shares soared by 7.3% in the session that followed—the best earnings reaction going back to January 2023, breaking a string of five consecutive moves lower post-reporting. Looking ahead to the Jan. 30 Q4 release, the options market prices in a somewhat elevated 4.5% earnings-related stock price swing when analyzing the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the reporting date. Implied volatility is back above 30%, while short interest on the stock is low at 1.42%.

Looking back on the quarter that was, AXP delivered strong Q3 results, driven by high card usage trends and despite higher operating expenses. Its Global Business Travel Group merger contributed modestly to the bottom line, while upbeat CEO commentary was something the bulls pointed to. Specifically, executives highlighted strong early momentum from the Platinum Card refresh (with its higher fees being most accretive to earnings in Q4 2026), including doubled account acquisitions and higher engagement. Billings accelerated broadly, especially in travel and entertainment, and share buybacks rose to $2.3 billion in the third quarter—a key part of the bulls’ thesis.

Solid consumer spending, particularly in travel and leisure, along with corporate trends, supported sales growth over the July through September period. Those trends appeared to continue through year-end, too, with early holiday spending up 9% YoY as of early December. We’ll get the full Q4 details at the end of this month.

On the call, the company reaffirmed its commitment to mid-teens EPS growth, even with short-term margin pressures from its investments. I’ll be on the lookout for details on how any proposed credit card interest rate cap may impact the company, which is more linked to higher-end consumers and the corporate space, versus the lower half of US consumers.

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to be strong in CY 2026 and through the out year. A solid low- to mid-teens bottom-line growth rate supports a market multiple, but I’ll note later that the P/E has gotten a bit stretched. Still, the sellside has turned much more upbeat, with 20 upgrades compared to just six downgrades.

Finally, American Express is among the most profitable Financials sector companies, now with free cash flow per share of $27.14 over the past 12 months.

American Express: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, I have key updates to my 2023 analysis. If we assume $18 of operating EPS over the next 12 months and apply a 21x P/E (above its 5-year average), then shares should trade near $378.

The earnings multiple is in line with the market’s, which I assert is about right, given its historical P/E trend both on an absolute basis and relative to the SPX. Shares also trade a bit rich on a price-to-sales basis.

AXP: Shares Have Become Fully Priced

Key risks for AXP include weaker spending trends among higher-wealth households, perhaps triggered by weaker equity markets this year. Moreover, a global macro slowdown could crimp enterprise and corporate travel spending—a key growth area for the company. Of course, if a 10% credit card interest rate cap is inked into law, that would prompt a significant operational change, and likely a higher risk premium.

For AXP specifically, we’ll see how the Platinum refresh is trending within the Q4 report and on the earnings call. Also, up-and-coming fintech players, including BNPL and crypto banking names, could represent a long-term competitive threat.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show a confirmed Q4 2025 earnings date of Friday, Jan. 30, BMO. Shares pay a $0.82 quarterly dividend on Tuesday, Feb. 10, the same day that the management team presents at the UBS Financial Services Conference 2026.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares now close to what I consider to be fair value, AXP’s technical situation is mixed. Notice in the chart below that while a clear series of higher highs and higher lows is in place, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph has printed lower highs. That's non-confirmation between momentum and price. I would not be surprised if AXP drifted lower into the Q4 report, eventually testing the rising long-term 200-day moving average later this year.

In the here and now, an uptrend support line is in play, currently near the $355 mark, while horizontal support is seen between $326 and $329. It’s important not to lose sight of the broader trend—up. Thus, it’s a "buy the dip" play if we do see a correction into the low-mid $300s. That would also mark a more favorable valuation long play. Big picture, a measured move upside price target to about $430 is in play based on the depth of the January to April decline and subsequent breakout.

AXP: Uptrend In Place, But Weaker RSI, Long-Term $430 Technical Target

The Bottom Line

I have a "Hold" rating on AXP. I see the credit card company’s shares as now close to fair value, while the chart sends mixed signals heading into the Q4 earnings report.

data.title: Critical Metals: Greenland Headlines And A Potential U.S. Stake Could Rerate It
data.description:
Critical Metals Corp. (CRML) is on a rollercoaster ride. Since rumors surfaced that the U.S. would take an equity stake in the company, shares tripled into the October highs, then sold off by 80% in the weeks after.

This year, the stock doubled after the U.S. interest in gaining control over Greenland showed no signs of slowing down.

Just like with MP Materials (MP) last year, fundamentals have little to do with the recent price action. Sentiment (mainly driven by headlines) is what's moving shares.

Overall, I see a few catalysts this year that could reignite shareholder enthusiasm (and therefore, a rally). First, the pilot plant in Qaqortoq is likely to be operational before the end of May. Second, the 300–500 kg/hour POC pilot plant in Romania is scheduled for Q2 this year. I admit that the upside from a fundamentals perspective will likely be limited this year. All I see is a possible improvement if the US takes a stake in the company. This could be the third catalyst to move shares, given the high capital needed to advance the Tanbrezz project in Greenland.

Finally, and this is a nice fat tail event, if the U.S. manages to strike a deal with Greenland (I don't believe annexation is likely), and if the terms include clauses for REEs, I believe CRML could see further upside (again, from a sentiment perspective).

Overall, I see CRML as a trading stock, not a long-term buy-and-hold opportunity. I rate it a cautious Buy, and I own a small position myself.

Business Overview And Recent Developments

CRML is a development-stage critical minerals company whose core value is the Tanbreez rare earth element project in Greenland.

It currently owns 42% (minority stake) of the Tanbreez project, so broadly, the bull case assumes one of the two scenarios:

  1. CRML monetizes its minority stake via future mine cash flows. To achieve this, they will need funding, which I don't think will be a big problem, as I will discuss in a moment.
  2. With time, CRML increases its ownership in the project. Back in September, the company announced intentions to step up from a minority stake to a controlling stake (92.5%) via an amended acquisition agreement with Rimbal, subject to Greenland approval. More on this topic in just a moment.

Earlier drilling results show relatively stable TREO grades at Tanbreez and a 25–27% heavy rare earth oxides share. This is great news, given that heavy REEs are typically scarcer and can be strategically valuable for magnets in defense applications.

The latest investor presentation cites a Tanbreez preliminary economic assessment, with NPV of US$3B, an IRR of 180%, and >US$26B revenue over a 25+ year mine life:

To convert the REE concentrate, the company recently announced a proposed processing route via a 50/50 JV with Romania’s state-owned Fabrica de Prelucrare a Concentratelor de Uraniu S.R.L. Plans include a 300–500 kg/hour POC pilot plant costing approximately $2 million, with commissioning expected in Q2 2026.

In line with this timeframe, the company announced that it has commenced construction of the Tanbreez pilot plant in Qaqortoq, with the pilot plant section targeted for no later than May 2026.

Now, not all concentrate from Tanbreez will be routed to Romania for post-processing. In the PR, CRML stated that only 50% of the Tanbreez concentrate will be sent to the Romania JV. The rest, I believe, will be sent to the US, given the Letter of Intent with Ucore Rare Metals Inc. (TSXV: UCU). For context, last year, CRML signed an LOI with Ucore for a 10-year offtake arrangement, including up to 10,000 metric tons annually of rare earth concentrate (about 10% of the initial projected Tanbreez production).

On top of that, the company secured an additional offtake agreement for 15% of production with REalloys.

Aside from REEs in Greenland, the company has exposure to a lithium project in Austria (Wolfsberg). That said, this project is currently on hold pending price recovery, and there are some regulatory items related to an environmental impact assessment, which add uncertainty to this project.

The Bull Case

First, from a fundamentals perspective, Tanbreez is still years away from revenue. Aside from the pilot plant in Qaqortoq this year (before May 2026), which I believe will bring some revenue in 2026, given the commissioning of the 300–500 kg/hr proof-of-concept pilot plant in Romania, the latest disclosed timeline implies mining starting in 2027 and first production around mid-2028.

As I hinted in the last section, Tanbreez is moving towards pilot production, with a target to have a pilot plant ready by May 2026. Well, it turns out that a high proportion of the planned output at Tanbreez is already secured. Management recently said that 75% of planned output is pre-sold, with the remaining 25% targeted for early 2026.

This is good news until one realizes the funding that is required. According to some sources, $500m in CapEx would be required in Greenland, plus another $1b for downstream processing facilities.

Therefore, the main pillar of the bull case is the company's success in securing this funding.

CRML has not published a newer balance sheet than June 30, 2025. Back then, cash on hand was $7.3m, and the net cash used in operating activities was $14.5 million.

Now, after the October 5 and 15 PIPE transactions, an F-3 form shows (adjusted) cash and cash equivalents of $87.2 million.

As a side note, the adjustment comes "after giving effect to the transactions contemplated by the Purchase Agreements" (i.e., the October 5 and October 15, 2025 PIPEs).

On top of that, the company secured an LOI from EXIM for up to $120 million as a 15-year term loan concept for Tanbreez. Some sources suggest that the project would need to be "well-capitalized with sufficient equity from strategic investors" before the loan would be approved. Which brings me to the next point.

Trump wants the U.S. to own Greenland for strategic reasons. In fact, during his first presidency, he publicly floated the idea of the U.S. buying Greenland. There is no doubt that there is interest in this area, not only from a geopolitical perspective, but also from a minerals perspective.

Back in October, Reuters reported talks to convert a $50M Defense Production Act (DPA) grant into an equity stake of roughly 8%, potentially structured with warrants. Even though $50M doesn't sound like a lot when compared to the $500M required for the Greenland project, such a development would improve the company's ability to secure more funding from other sources (like the $120M loan with EXIM).

On top of that, an investment by the U.S. government would drive up the sentiment around this stock. Just take a look at MP Material's price action after the US announced an equity investment in July last year.

Risks

It is my view that the stock is being bid on geopolitics and strategic headlines.

A clear example of what happens when the headlines are overtaking fundamentals is MP Materials. After the outperformance that I discussed in the last section, the stock sold off 50% between its highs in October and December 31, 2025.

I warn readers that a similar selloff is likely for CRML, especially if the headline cadence slows down.

As you may know, the stock was down close to 80% after the highs in October, following the rumor of the U.S. equity stake in the company.

The company doesn't have any meaningful valuation ratios given its early stage. All I'm able to do is a rough estimate of the cash runway. Using the $87M from the latest F-3 and a cash burn of $21.2M ($15M in net cash used in operating activities and $7M in net cash used in investing activities), I obtain a runway of 49 months (4.1 years). I believe this estimate is not realistic, given the $500M estimated to advance the Tanbreez mine in Greenland. To achieve this, the company needs external funding. A U.S. stake could be favorable (as it will give credibility to other loan providers), but it is unclear if it will eventually materialize.

Conclusion

In my view, two milestones will determine whether the Tanbreez bull case converts from a narrative into an executable build plan.

The project needs roughly $500M in Greenland CapEx plus $1B for downstream facilities, far beyond the current $87.2M pro forma cash.

The company needs to raise capital via the $120M EXIM term-loan and any U.S. participation, including the reported $50M DPA grant conversion discussion. Until then, dilution remains a key risk to monitor, especially after the stock is up over 100% since the start of the year.

The other milestone is related to the delivery of the Qaqortoq pilot plant section by May 2026 and commissioning of the 300–500 kg/hour Romania POC pilot in Q2 2026. Even though I expect minimal revenue contribution this year, I believe it is important to show progress on the execution side. Any delays could hurt sentiment, and I believe sentiment is what's driving the share price right now.

Overall, I'm bullish given that I believe the U.S. will (at least) make a deal with Greenland. I am unsure if its annexation will happen, but an REE deal is likely given the recent interest in this region.

data.title: Madness And Mayhem Continue In January
data.description:
Happy Monday!

Heading into the traditionally challenging second year of the US presidential cycle, global news flow continues to erode consumer and business confidence. In response, the Trump administration is intensifying efforts to boost popular support through handouts and distractions. In the early going this morning, equity futures are down, along with the US dollar.

Among the top headlines yesterday, President Trump directed the US Department of Justice to serve the Federal Reserve with grand jury subpoenas, threatening a criminal indictment over Chairman Jay Powell’s congressional testimony last June regarding the ongoing renovation of the central bank’s headquarters.

In a written and video statement released Sunday evening, Fed Chair Powell took the gloves off, saying:

“The threat of criminal charges is a consequence of the Federal Reserve setting interest rates based on our best assessment of what will serve the public, rather than following the preferences of the president.”

Meanwhile, layoffs are continuing at a pace as tariffs and weaker demand bite into profit margins. Companies of all stripes are filing for bankruptcy; large corporate bankruptcies are at their highest level in 15 years. See, Bankruptcies are exploding across the economy, hitting small businesses and households. Few industries are immune:
